sentences.text: Well, I was-- I was too shy, almost, to get married.
He had two younger brothers, which really, physically, had to drag me to the wedding.
Well, I liked-- he was older than I am.
Him was maybe 12 years older than I am-- I was.
And I don't know why.
I don't know why.
I just got married.
I liked the guy.
No.
My parents were in Konskie.
That's correct.
Yeah.
And he really was working under the Russian as a painter-- daytime.
They had an atelier with a couple of other fellows.
And in the evening, he was playing the violin in orchestra.
And he had wonderful two parents-- mother and father-- very, very fine people.
And I-- after I got married, I lived with them.
And I used to go to visit him in the atelier.
And his father always, every lunchtime, used to bring him the sandwich.
And it was OK.
No.
No, nothing, nothing.
Yes.
That lasted-- I got married in '41.
And one-- I was-- I was three months married when the Germans came in-- three month.
And before the three months, there was-- everybody knew the Germans are approaching.
So they start-- a lot of people from Ostrog ran deep into Russia to run from the Germans.
And they begged my husband to run.
He said, he's not leaving his older parents.
So he stayed on.
With you?
With me, and his parents, and his two brothers.
Then the Germans came, and then the saga started.
From the very beginning, they organized a Jewish committee, which was-- from-- they-- they picked the-- really, the intelligentsia of Ostrog to be in this committee-- some doctors, lawyers.
And every day, we had other requests for things.
There was another list in the committee hanging what delivery the Jews have today, to do that particular day.
One day was money.
One day was boots.
One day was leather.
One day was-- every day, something else.
And every day, they also requested some people for work, which never came back.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
My brother?
Yes.
In [NON-ENGLISH]?
Yes.
A big difference.
You mean the committee under the Germans?
Big difference, [LAUGHS] because this committee was forced on them.
Nobody wanted to do it, really.
They just wanted to get organized how to request things from all the Jews.
There was no ghetto.
All the Jews were spread around the town.
They needed somebody to hold responsible for all those things.
Yeah.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Whoever had anything, they had to bring, because that was, at that time, everybody thought that's a way of survival.
There was no ghetto at that time, as I said before.
That was for one month.
We were-- everybody was scared, because there were always rumors that they will take people to work.
It was a terrible, terrible panic.
One month after they came in, they-- they guarded.
That was the first raid-- organized raid-- by the SS, plus the Ukrainian militzia.
I mean, everybody expected something.
But that was towards the morning.
It was still dark.
Yeah.
They broke the doors.
They came in like animals with the machine guns-- literally took us out from beds.
I don't think-- I don't remember if I had the chance to put on a pair of shoes.
Everybody was out, since the SS didn't know where the Jews lived.
Because there was no ghetto at that time.
The local militzia, the Ukrainian militzia did the dirty job for the SS, taking them from one Jewish house to another Jewish house.
They knew everyone.
And they guarded us in the middle of the street.
Everybody.
Every-- sick people from hospitals.
I remember we had very good friends which owned a drug store in the town.
And they had two sons.
Their mother was in the hospital.
And they had to carry their mother on a stretcher.
So that was-- they guarded all the Jews in the middle of the street.
They guarded us on the sides with the machine guns.
They kept us in line.
Because there were-- people was-- were wild.
There were such a screamings, and cryings, and praying.
So they-- when they got out of line, the machine guns pushed us back into line.
It's like live cattle.
And they marched us like this maybe for three miles.
And it was a terrible, hot day.
No.
Nothing.
All-- all you heard was raus, raus, and raus, and the machine guns.
They marched us for three miles to the outskirts of the city where it was a big, big, place.
And then it really started.
It was so hot.
We didn't have any water-- no food, nothing.
And they put us in lines.
If I remember well, when we arrived there, we saw a big hole digged out and some SS lying on their bellies around the hole with the machine guns, like this, pulled out.
In the meantime, they put us in lines on this-- in this place.
And they started pointing their finger-- one.
They took us-- the younger men, and separated them to a separate line.
They took him.
No, no-- the parents.
They were old.
They took only--
--one.
Only him, they took.
One of the brothers-- I forgot to mention-- one of his brothers was in the-- went to the Russian army.
So he wasn't there.
I-- Russian-- he might be still alive.
I don't know.
He went with the other-- Russian army.
So it was only one brother left.
No.
Only my husband.
Pardon me?
How old?
Yeah.
Yeah.
And they made a separate completely line of those people that they selected.
We had a German which was patrolling us.
He was going from one end to another.
And he spoke a little bit Polish.
And he really tried to help.

Because he was going from one end to another, and he was saying in Polish, 'Here is life; over there is death,' pointing to the-- to the people which they selected, to the younger people over there they took out.
Yes.
So he was walking back and forth saying, 'Here is life; over there is death,' which means he knew they were selected to be killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this saga was going on the whole day with screaming.
Oh.
When I heard-- when he told us that here is life; over there is death, automatically, not thinking much, I ran to the middle of this big place where all the big SS was there.
And I don't even know what language I used.
I begged them to send him back.
What I got just was a kick in my behind.
And they told me to go back.
No.
My brother, at that time, went also to Russia.
Yes.
Well, I'm--
I'm coming to it.
They kept us the whole day like this, from early morning in hot sun-- no food, no drink, you know, cold.
And they separated 3,000 young men.
They left them behind.
The rest was going back to the town with the same German which patrolled us.
Until today, I cannot forget-- I saw him crying.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Oh, sure.
Yes.
So I saw him crying.
And he took us back to the town.
So we really didn't have any source of food or nothing.
Yes-- with his two old parents and his brother.
But the parents were very concerned about me.
They were so afraid that the Germans may-- they were-- used to take from the street young people and send them off.
So they didn't let me go out from the house.
They went.
They took some clothes.
And they went outside of the town to exchange for some food.
After this incident-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --and that was another month passed like this.
No.
But the peasant tried to cash in on it.
They used to come every day with some other news that they saw one man here, one man there-- that blackmail-- just to get something out.
Nobody, of course, came.
While-- while we were going back from this place, we heard the shootings.
Yes.
So we knew that they were shooting them, and this hole was prepared.
No.
He was-- they shot him there.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
Events-- yeah.
Yes, and that's how we survived.
Then--
One month later, exactly to the date, there was another raid.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Wait a moment.
After this first raid, they created the ghetto.
I stayed in the part, which was the Jewish part, where they-- all the Jews supposed to come there, to this part of the town.
Yes.
Yes.
I didn't have to move.
But then a month later, while I was visiting a relative in the ghetto, there was another raid.
I tried to think, because in the meantime, after they created the ghetto-- I have to go back.
After they created the ghetto, I don't know how I was picked.
They left in the town three Germans to supervise the business of the town-- so-called three gendarmes-- you know, with the green helmets and-- and I was chosen to-- to keep household in their house.
Yeah, for those three.
They had an house, an apartment, and I was chosen.
I don't remember details how I was chosen to go in the morning there, out from the ghetto, to clean, to cook.
I didn't have any idea of cooking at all.
I needed help from the Jewish committee.
Women used to come and help me, because I didn't know how to cook.
So I went there in the morning and came in the evening.
At least I could bring some food in.
They let me.
I had a special pass to go out to work in the morning.
When I came in the evening, I could bring from the Germans.
They were-- you know-- they weren't SS.
They were some regular Germans, and didn't mind me bringing any of the food.
They were correct.
Usually, when I came in the evening home, my house was full of people, because they always thought that I get the news-- what's going to happen.
Everybody was waiting for something.
We lived on rumors from minute to minute.
So they always thought that I bring some news what's going to happen.
They didn't tell me anything.
They were correct, pleasant to me.
But they never told me there's going to be another raid, which happened a month after.
This one month, going back and forth, at least I could feed my, you know, in-laws.
Then to the date, one month later, I was visiting my-- a relative-- friend-- she's now in Israel-- her household.
And I was sitting there-- daytime.
And we heard the loud speakers. 'Everybody come out to work.
We need some workers.' This girl whom I visited, she had an intuition or something.
She said, let's not go.
They had in their house a Ukrainian lady, which used to clean their house for many years.
She was staying in the kitchen washing laundry.
I remember like now.
She was-- you know, there-- it-- there was not a washing machine at that time.
She was washing the laundry on a-- how do you call it?
Washboard.
Washboard-- a older lady.
And she was staying over there, washing and washing.
And this girl, my friend, said, let's not go out.
I don't like to go.
So they had an attic.
There was a square in the wall which lead to an attic-- not high, just one step ladder could make it.
So she said, let's hide there.
And I went after her-- luckily.
Otherwise, I wouldn't be able to tell this.
We went both out on the attic.
Her parents went.
Her younger sister went.
And we were sitting up there.
And you can imagine what fear.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was-- the hall wasn't even covered.
It was open completely.
You pulled-- we pulled the ladder up with us.
And we're sitting there.
And we just heard, every 10 minutes, another Ukrainian patrol coming in, talking to the woman.
Is anybody here?
She said, no.
She didn't give us away.
She knew?
She knew, of course.
She saw us going up there.
She didn't give us away.
She hardly was talking.
She was-- she had this scarf over her head.
She was mumbling.
One patrol was out.
The other one came in, looking around.
And we were sitting up there with a opening.
And nobody looked up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, there was one miracle after another.
I don't know who was upstairs watching over me.
When the whole thing finished, we went down from there.
Nobody came back from this raid.
They killed them all.
They took them into trucks-- her parents, her youngest sister-- beautiful girl.
They killed them all.
Nobody came back.
Nobody.
Yes.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
From that point on here, it's a very important thing to mention-- that it was known about a company called Jung-- J-U-N-G.
In English, it would be young.
But they-- there was Jung firma-- German-- which was a private company-- building company.
They were building storages for the Germans to keep the wheat, which was collected from the fields.
And they-- they have a lot of Jews working for them.
This company had a chain.
They were in Ostrog.
They were in nearby Ozhenin, a little town.
They were in a bigger town, Zdolbunow And they were in town, Rovno.
And they were in some place deep in the Ukraine.
And in Ozhenin, there was a group of Jews from Ostrog, which was working for this firm.
In Ostrog were very few Jews left.
So I decided to go to Ozhenin.
I was able to do it.
I was very near-- maybe an hour, 1/2 an hour or so from there, from Ostrog.
So I went to Ozhenin.
And I worked there for a while.
In the kitchen.
The head of this-- of this company, in Ozhenin, was a German, older German, from Hamburg-- tall.
Well, he was maybe 70 or so.
And he was the head of this group of workers in Ozhenin.
And he-- but the Germans knew about it too, because they came there with a truck, collect the people from there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this German took a liking to me.
And he always told me, you know, they will take everybody from here, but not you.
So any-- he knew in advance that the truck is coming.
He sent me out to the village to sit out this day.
And after that, I came back.
And that was twice a truckload of people went from Ozhenin.
They never came back.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
His name was Groebe-- G-R-O-E-B-E-- Groebe-- Herman Groebe.
By the way, he is on the righteous--
Gentiles there, yeah.
He helped a lot.
So when I saw there were fewer and fewer of us, the last truck has to take everybody.
I knew that his headquarters are in Zdolbunow-- Z-D-O-L-B-U-N-O-W-- Zdolbunow.
It was a bigger town.
So I made my way there to look for him, because they really-- the days were counted when they will liquidate everybody.
And you were all alone by then?
Yes.
So I made my-- I don't remember how I made my way to Zdolbunow.
I think walking.
Maybe some horses picked me up on the way, you know, with the carriage from the peasants-- gave me a little ride.
But that was probably also an hour from Ozhenin.
You walked with or without the Jewish star?
Without.
You took-- I took off.
I was walking.
I was walking, I remember, in a dress that I just had on me, and a pair of sandals-- that's all.
I arrived to Zdolbunow in the evening, and I hear the shootings all around.
I asked for the headquarters of this company, and in-- in-- Groebe.
I wanted to see Groebe.
Somebody showed me to the place.
And I-- as I said before, I was so lucky.
I came into his office, and he was there.
It really-- it-- it sounds like it's not real.
When I told him I'm Jewish, I'm running, and I need help, he didn't even hesitate for a second.
He called one of his drivers.
The driver came with a-- not car-- a open trunk.
And I was-- he took me to a building where those people from the company, the Germans from the company, lived.
Yes.
The-- the quarters of theirs where they were sleeping there.
And he opened one of the rooms.
He let me into the room.
He left.
He put the lock outside.
And I was there the whole night.
Alone-- while all this shooting was going on around me.
[LAUGHS]
Tell me about it.
Anyway, I was crying the whole night.
Later on, I found out that the liquidation of the ghetto in Zdolbunow was going on that night.
Yes.
OK.
